questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presents for a routine evaluation. During cardiac catheterization, left ventricular pressure tracings are obtained. The aortic valve opens when left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic diastolic pressure, and the mitral valve closes shortly after the onset of ventricular contraction. During the interval between mitral valve closure and aortic valve opening, which of the following best describes the changes occurring in the left ventricle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Volume decreases while pressure increases rapidly

choices.label: B
choices.text: Volume decreases while pressure remains relatively constant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Volume remains constant while pressure decreases rapidly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Volume increases while pressure increases gradually

choices.label: E
choices.text: Volume remains constant while pressure increases rapidly

explanation.summary: The interval between mitral valve closure and aortic valve opening defines isovolumetric contraction, during which ventricular volume is constant (all valves closed) while pressure rises rapidly.
explanation.whyCorrect: During isovolumetric contraction, both the mitral and aortic valves are closed, creating a sealed chamber. The ventricle contracts against a fixed volume of blood, causing a rapid rise in intraventricular pressure without any change in volume. This phase ends when LV pressure exceeds aortic diastolic pressure and the aortic valve opens.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Volume does not decrease during isovolumetric contraction because both valves are closed and no blood is ejected from the ventricle during this phase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pressure increases, not decreases, during isovolumetric contraction because the ventricle is actively contracting against a closed chamber of blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Volume does not increase during this phase; ventricular filling has already occurred during diastole and the mitral valve is now closed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Volume does not decrease and pressure does not remain constant; the ventricle is contracting isovolumetrically with a rapid pressure rise.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man undergoes cardiac catheterization after presenting with exertional dyspnea. Simultaneous left ventricular and aortic pressure tracings are recorded. At one point during the cardiac cycle, the left ventricular pressure falls below the aortic pressure, and the aortic valve closes. The dicrotic notch is observed on the aortic pressure tracing at this moment. Which of the following events occurs immediately after this point?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid ventricular filling begins as the mitral valve opens

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isovolumetric contraction begins with rapid pressure rise

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial contraction contributes the final portion of filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isovolumetric relaxation occurs with all valves closed

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rapid ventricular ejection occurs as blood enters the aorta

explanation.summary: The dicrotic notch marks aortic valve closure, which initiates isovolumetric relaxation. During this phase, all four valves are closed and ventricular pressure drops rapidly without a change in volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: After aortic valve closure (marked by the dicrotic notch), the ventricle enters isovolumetric relaxation. Both the aortic and mitral valves are closed, so ventricular volume remains constant while the myocardium relaxes and pressure drops rapidly. This continues until LV pressure falls below left atrial pressure and the mitral valve opens.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rapid ventricular filling occurs after isovolumetric relaxation, once ventricular pressure drops below atrial pressure and the mitral valve opens, not immediately after aortic valve closure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial contraction (the atrial kick) occurs late in diastole, well after the mitral valve has already opened and passive filling has occurred.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Isovolumetric contraction occurs at the beginning of systole, after the mitral valve closes, not after aortic valve closure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rapid ventricular ejection occurs during systole when the aortic valve is open, not after it has already closed at the dicrotic notch.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with compensated heart failure is being evaluated with a Swan-Ganz catheter. His heart rate is 80 bpm with a cardiac output of 5.6 L/min. Analysis of the left atrial pressure tracing reveals three positive waves. The physician notes that the 'a' wave occurs just before the first heart sound. Which of the following physiological events is responsible for generating the 'a' wave in the left atrial pressure tracing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Active contraction of the left atrium against an open mitral valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bulging of the mitral valve into the atrium during systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passive filling of the atrium from pulmonary venous return

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sudden opening of the mitral valve after isovolumetric relaxation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Retrograde transmission of the ventricular pressure into the atrium

explanation.summary: The 'a' wave in the atrial pressure tracing is produced by atrial contraction (atrial systole), which actively pushes blood into the ventricle through the open AV valve just before ventricular systole.
explanation.whyCorrect: The 'a' wave reflects the increase in atrial pressure caused by active atrial contraction. This occurs in late diastole, just before S1 and mitral valve closure. The atrium contracts against an open mitral valve, contributing approximately 15-25% of ventricular filling (the 'atrial kick').

whyWrongByChoice.C: Passive filling of the atrium from venous return produces the 'v' wave, not the 'a' wave. The 'v' wave occurs during ventricular systole when the mitral valve is closed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bulging of the closed mitral valve into the atrium during early ventricular systole produces the 'c' wave, not the 'a' wave.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Opening of the mitral valve causes the 'y' descent (fall in atrial pressure as blood drains into the ventricle), not the 'a' wave.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Retrograde pressure transmission does not normally produce the 'a' wave; this mechanism is seen pathologically in conditions such as tricuspid regurgitation producing giant 'v' waves.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation undergoes right heart catheterization. The physician notes an absence of a specific wave on the right atrial pressure tracing that is normally present in patients with sinus rhythm. Which of the following hemodynamic consequences is most likely observed in this patient compared to a similar patient in normal sinus rhythm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased end-diastolic volume due to loss of atrial contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure from valvular obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated central venous pressure due to increased venous resistance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased stroke volume due to prolonged isovolumetric contraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased ejection fraction due to compensatory ventricular contraction

explanation.summary: In atrial fibrillation, organized atrial contraction is lost, eliminating the 'a' wave from the atrial pressure tracing and reducing end-diastolic volume due to loss of the atrial kick.
explanation.whyCorrect: In atrial fibrillation, the atria fibrillate rather than contract in an organized fashion. This eliminates the 'a' wave and the 'atrial kick,' which normally contributes 15-25% of ventricular filling. The result is decreased end-diastolic volume and, by the Frank-Starling mechanism, decreased stroke volume.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Atrial fibrillation does not cause valvular obstruction; increased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure in AF is related to loss of organized atrial contraction, not mechanical obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Central venous pressure may increase in AF but not primarily due to increased venous resistance; the mechanism relates to loss of coordinated atrial contraction and impaired atrial emptying.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The duration of isovolumetric contraction is not significantly prolonged in atrial fibrillation; the primary deficit is reduced ventricular filling from loss of atrial contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ejection fraction typically decreases or remains unchanged in atrial fibrillation because stroke volume decreases with reduced preload, not increases.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is being monitored during an exercise stress test. At baseline, his heart rate is 70 bpm. At peak exercise, his heart rate increases to 160 bpm. The cardiologist explains that the duration of the cardiac cycle is shortened primarily by reducing one specific phase. Which phase of the cardiac cycle is most shortened during tachycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isovolumetric contraction phase of ventricular systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid ejection phase of ventricular systole during early systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial systole phase occurring just before ventricular contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isovolumetric relaxation phase occurring after aortic valve closure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diastolic filling period including rapid and slow filling phases

explanation.summary: During tachycardia, diastole is shortened more than systole. The diastolic filling period, particularly the slow filling phase (diastasis), is the phase most reduced in duration.
explanation.whyCorrect: When heart rate increases, the total cardiac cycle duration decreases. Diastole is disproportionately shortened because systole has a relatively fixed duration determined by the intrinsic properties of the action potential and contraction. The slow filling phase (diastasis) is virtually eliminated at high heart rates, and rapid filling time is also reduced.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Isovolumetric contraction is a brief phase that remains relatively constant in duration regardless of heart rate changes due to the fixed nature of ventricular depolarization and early contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The rapid ejection phase is shortened slightly during tachycardia, but diastole is shortened to a much greater extent than any systolic phase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Isovolumetric relaxation time is modestly shortened during tachycardia, but this is a relatively brief phase and not the primary phase shortened during increased heart rates.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial systole is shortened during tachycardia but represents a smaller absolute reduction compared to the marked shortening of the overall diastolic filling period.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with chronic stable angina undergoes cardiac catheterization. The left ventricular pressure-volume loop is obtained. The physician identifies four corners of the loop corresponding to valve events. Point A represents mitral valve closure, Point B represents aortic valve opening, Point C represents aortic valve closure, and Point D represents mitral valve opening. Which segment of the pressure-volume loop represents the phase during which the ventricle performs the greatest external work?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Segment A to B representing isovolumetric contraction phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Segments A to B and C to D combined representing isovolumetric phases

choices.label: C
choices.text: Segment C to D representing isovolumetric relaxation phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Segment D to A representing the ventricular filling phase

choices.label: E
choices.text: Segment B to C representing the ventricular ejection phase

explanation.summary: The ventricular ejection phase (B to C) is when the ventricle performs external work by ejecting blood against aortic pressure. The area enclosed by the PV loop represents stroke work.
explanation.whyCorrect: During the ejection phase (aortic valve opening to aortic valve closure), the ventricle actively ejects blood into the aorta against systemic vascular resistance. This is when the greatest external (stroke) work is performed, as work equals the integral of pressure times change in volume. The ventricle shortens, volume decreases, and blood is propelled into the circulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: During isovolumetric contraction, pressure rises but volume does not change; since work is pressure times change in volume, no external work is performed during this phase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: During isovolumetric relaxation, pressure decreases but volume remains constant; no external work is performed as there is no volume change and no blood ejection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: During ventricular filling, the ventricle receives blood passively and from atrial contraction; the ventricle does not perform significant external work during this phase.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Both isovolumetric phases involve no volume change by definition, so no external work is performed during either of these phases despite pressure changes.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with worsening dyspnea. An ECG shows normal sinus rhythm. The P wave on the ECG corresponds to atrial depolarization. On the left ventricular volume curve obtained during catheterization, a small additional increase in ventricular volume is observed just before the onset of ventricular systole. Which of the following best explains the timing relationship between the P wave and this volume change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The P wave occurs simultaneously with the final increase in ventricular volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: The P wave occurs during ventricular ejection and is unrelated to volume changes

choices.label: C
choices.text: The P wave follows the volume increase because atrial filling causes depolarization

choices.label: D
choices.text: The P wave precedes the volume increase because electrical activity precedes contraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: The P wave follows isovolumetric contraction and coincides with aortic valve opening

explanation.summary: The P wave (atrial depolarization) precedes the mechanical event of atrial contraction due to electromechanical coupling delay, so it occurs before the atrial kick causes the final increase in ventricular volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: Electrical depolarization (the P wave) must precede mechanical contraction of the atria. After the P wave triggers atrial depolarization, there is a brief delay for excitation-contraction coupling before the atria contract and push additional blood into the ventricles (atrial kick). Therefore, the P wave precedes the small increase in ventricular volume seen in late diastole.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The P wave does not occur simultaneously with the volume increase; there is always an electromechanical delay between depolarization and the resulting mechanical contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The P wave represents atrial depolarization, which is an electrical event that initiates contraction; it does not follow the mechanical event of volume change.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The P wave occurs during late diastole (before ventricular systole), not during ventricular ejection; it is directly related to the atrial kick volume change.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The P wave occurs before ventricular systole begins, well before isovolumetric contraction and aortic valve opening, which are ventricular systolic events.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with newly diagnosed heart failure has a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35%. His end-diastolic volume is measured at 200 mL. Assuming his end-systolic volume is proportionally increased, which of the following is the closest estimate of his stroke volume?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 50 mL, reflecting severely impaired systolic performance

choices.label: B
choices.text: 70 mL, reflecting moderately impaired systolic performance

choices.label: C
choices.text: 90 mL, reflecting mildly impaired systolic performance

choices.label: D
choices.text: 110 mL, reflecting near-normal systolic performance

choices.label: E
choices.text: 130 mL, reflecting preserved systolic performance at rest

explanation.summary: Stroke volume = EDV x EF = 200 mL x 0.35 = 70 mL. Despite the dilated ventricle (EDV 200 mL), the reduced EF (35%) results in a moderately reduced stroke volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ejection fraction is defined as stroke volume divided by end-diastolic volume (SV/EDV). Therefore, SV = EF x EDV = 0.35 x 200 mL = 70 mL. The end-systolic volume would be 200 - 70 = 130 mL. This demonstrates how ventricular dilation partially compensates for reduced contractility via the Frank-Starling mechanism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A stroke volume of 50 mL would correspond to an ejection fraction of 25% (50/200), which is lower than the stated 35% ejection fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A stroke volume of 90 mL would correspond to an ejection fraction of 45% (90/200), which is higher than the stated 35% ejection fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A stroke volume of 110 mL would correspond to an ejection fraction of 55% (110/200), which represents normal systolic function, not the stated 35%.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A stroke volume of 130 mL would correspond to an ejection fraction of 65% (130/200), which is above normal and inconsistent with the clinical scenario.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man undergoes cardiac catheterization. The aortic pressure tracing shows a peak systolic pressure of 130 mmHg and a dicrotic notch at 90 mmHg. The left ventricular pressure tracing shows a peak pressure of 130 mmHg. During the reduced ejection phase, which of the following correctly describes the relationship between left ventricular and aortic pressures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure throughout this phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure throughout this phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aortic pressure initially exceeds, then falls below ventricular pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both pressures remain equal throughout this entire phase of ejection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left ventricular pressure initially exceeds, then falls below aortic pressure

explanation.summary: During reduced ejection, ventricular pressure begins to fall as contraction wanes. LV pressure drops below aortic pressure, but ejection continues briefly due to the momentum of blood flow until the aortic valve closes.
explanation.whyCorrect: During the reduced ejection phase, myocardial contraction is waning and the ventricle begins to relax. Initially, LV pressure may still slightly exceed aortic pressure, but it progressively falls below aortic pressure. Remarkably, forward flow and ejection continue briefly because of the kinetic energy (momentum) of the blood column, even after the pressure gradient reverses. The aortic valve closes when flow reversal occurs, producing the dicrotic notch.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left ventricular pressure does not exceed aortic pressure throughout the reduced ejection phase; it falls below aortic pressure as ventricular relaxation begins.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic pressure does not exceed ventricular pressure for the entire reduced ejection phase; at the start, ventricular pressure may still be slightly higher or equal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pressures are not equal throughout; the hallmark of reduced ejection is a developing pressure gradient favoring the aorta as the ventricle relaxes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This reverses the actual sequence; ventricular pressure starts higher and then drops below aortic pressure, not the other way around.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is being evaluated by a cardiologist. During the cardiac cycle, the mitral valve opens when left atrial pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure. The jugular venous pulse tracing shows a descent that corresponds to this event. Which of the following waves or descents in the jugular venous pulse tracing occurs at the time of mitral valve opening?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The 'a' wave corresponding to atrial contraction during late diastole

choices.label: B
choices.text: The 'x' descent corresponding to atrial relaxation during ventricular systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: The 'c' wave corresponding to bulging of the tricuspid valve into the atrium

choices.label: D
choices.text: The 'y' descent corresponding to atrial emptying into the ventricle

choices.label: E
choices.text: The 'v' wave corresponding to passive atrial filling during ventricular systole

explanation.summary: The 'y' descent on the jugular venous pulse tracing reflects the fall in atrial pressure as blood drains from the atrium into the ventricle after AV valve opening.
explanation.whyCorrect: The 'y' descent occurs when the tricuspid (or mitral on the left side) valve opens at the end of isovolumetric relaxation. Blood rapidly flows from the atrium into the ventricle, causing a drop in atrial pressure. This decline in pressure is represented as the 'y' descent on the JVP tracing. The timing corresponds to the onset of rapid ventricular filling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The 'a' wave occurs during atrial contraction in late diastole, which is well after the AV valves have already been open and passive filling has occurred.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The 'x' descent occurs during ventricular systole and reflects atrial relaxation combined with downward displacement of the tricuspid annulus, not AV valve opening.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The 'c' wave occurs at the onset of ventricular systole when the tricuspid valve bulges into the atrium during isovolumetric contraction, well before AV valve opening.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The 'v' wave represents passive atrial filling while the AV valve is still closed during ventricular systole; the peak of the 'v' wave occurs just before AV valve opening.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man undergoes simultaneous recording of an ECG and left ventricular pressure tracing during cardiac catheterization. The physician notes that the QRS complex on the ECG precedes the rapid upstroke in left ventricular pressure. A medical student asks when the aortic valve opens relative to the ECG. Which of the following best describes the timing of aortic valve opening on the ECG?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During the P wave, before atrial contraction has fully completed

choices.label: B
choices.text: At the peak of the R wave, simultaneous with ventricular depolarization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shortly after the QRS complex, following isovolumetric contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: At the end of the T wave, corresponding to ventricular repolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: During the PR interval, between atrial and ventricular depolarization

explanation.summary: The aortic valve opens after isovolumetric contraction, which begins after ventricular depolarization (QRS). Therefore, aortic valve opening occurs shortly after the QRS complex.
explanation.whyCorrect: The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization, which triggers ventricular contraction. After the QRS, the ventricle enters isovolumetric contraction (all valves closed, pressure rising). Once LV pressure exceeds aortic diastolic pressure, the aortic valve opens. This occurs shortly after the QRS complex, typically during the early ST segment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The P wave corresponds to atrial depolarization, which occurs well before ventricular systole and aortic valve opening; the ventricle is still in diastole during this time.
whyWrongByChoice.B: At the peak of the R wave, ventricular depolarization is occurring but isovolumetric contraction has not yet generated sufficient pressure to open the aortic valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The end of the T wave corresponds to the end of ventricular repolarization, by which time systolic ejection is nearly complete and the aortic valve is about to close.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The PR interval represents conduction through the AV node; the ventricle has not yet depolarized or begun contracting during this interval.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with suspected diastolic dysfunction undergoes echocardiography. The E/A ratio of the mitral inflow Doppler is measured. The 'E' wave represents early passive filling, and the 'A' wave represents late filling from atrial contraction. In a healthy 25-year-old individual, which of the following E/A ratio patterns would be expected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: E/A ratio less than 0.8, indicating predominant atrial contribution to filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: E/A ratio approximately equal to 1.0, indicating equal contributions throughout

choices.label: C
choices.text: E/A ratio greater than 1.5, indicating predominant early passive filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: E/A ratio close to 0.5, indicating significant impairment of passive filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: E/A ratio greater than 3.0, indicating restrictive physiology and elevated pressures

explanation.summary: In healthy young adults, early passive filling (E wave) predominates over atrial contraction (A wave), producing an E/A ratio greater than 1.5, reflecting normal ventricular compliance and relaxation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a healthy young person, the ventricle relaxes rapidly and has high compliance, creating a strong suction effect that draws blood in during early diastole. This produces a tall E wave. The atrial contraction (A wave) contributes a smaller proportion because the ventricle is already well-filled. The normal E/A ratio in young adults is typically 1.5-2.0.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An E/A ratio less than 0.8 indicates impaired relaxation, which is seen in early diastolic dysfunction or normal aging, not in a healthy 25-year-old.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An E/A ratio of approximately 1.0 can represent a 'pseudonormal' pattern seen in moderate diastolic dysfunction or normal aging, not the pattern of a healthy young adult.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An E/A ratio of 0.5 indicates severely impaired relaxation with predominant atrial filling, suggesting significant diastolic dysfunction, not normal physiology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An E/A ratio greater than 3.0 indicates restrictive filling physiology with markedly elevated left atrial pressures, seen in severe diastolic dysfunction or restrictive cardiomyopathy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents for a sports physical. On cardiac auscultation, the physician hears the first heart sound (S1). The patient asks what produces this sound. Which of the following most accurately describes the mechanism that generates S1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Closure of the aortic and pulmonic valves at the end of ventricular systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turbulent flow of blood across the aortic valve during rapid ventricular ejection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves at the onset of ventricular systole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid deceleration of blood during the early rapid filling phase of diastole

choices.label: E
choices.text: Opening of the aortic and pulmonic valves during isovolumetric contraction phase

explanation.summary: S1 is produced by the closure of the mitral and tricuspid (atrioventricular) valves at the beginning of ventricular systole, when rising ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: S1 is generated by the closure of the AV valves (mitral and tricuspid) at the onset of ventricular systole. As the ventricle begins to contract and intraventricular pressure rises above atrial pressure, the AV valves snap shut. The mitral component (M1) normally precedes the tricuspid component (T1) slightly because left ventricular pressure rises faster.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Closure of the aortic and pulmonic (semilunar) valves produces S2, not S1. This occurs at the end of ventricular systole when ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Turbulent flow across valves produces murmurs, not normal heart sounds. Normal ejection through the aortic valve is typically laminar and does not produce an audible sound.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rapid deceleration of blood during early filling is the mechanism for S3, a low-pitched sound heard in early diastole, not S1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Semilunar valve opening is normally a silent event; S1 occurs before aortic valve opening, during the isovolumetric contraction phase.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is examined during a routine checkup. On auscultation, the physician notes that S2 splits during inspiration and becomes single during expiration. Which of the following best explains the physiological mechanism of this normal (physiological) splitting of S2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased venous return during inspiration delays mitral valve closure significantly

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased left ventricular afterload during inspiration causes earlier aortic closure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased right ventricular contractility during inspiration delays pulmonic closure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased right ventricular filling during inspiration delays pulmonic valve closure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased left ventricular filling during inspiration delays aortic valve closure

explanation.summary: During inspiration, negative intrathoracic pressure increases venous return to the right heart, increasing RV stroke volume and prolonging RV ejection time, thereby delaying pulmonic valve closure and widening the A2-P2 interval.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inspiration generates more negative intrathoracic pressure, which increases venous return to the right ventricle. The increased RV preload leads to a larger stroke volume and prolonged RV ejection time. This delays pulmonic valve closure (P2) relative to aortic valve closure (A2), producing audible splitting of S2. Additionally, pooling of blood in the pulmonary vasculature slightly reduces LV filling, causing earlier A2.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Inspiration does not delay mitral valve closure; mitral valve closure occurs at the onset of systole (S1), not during S2. Additionally, inspiration actually slightly decreases left ventricular filling.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While intrathoracic pressure changes can affect afterload, the primary mechanism of physiological splitting is increased RV preload delaying P2, not changes in LV afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inspiration actually decreases left ventricular filling (due to blood pooling in the pulmonary vasculature), which causes earlier rather than later aortic valve closure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RV contractility is not decreased during inspiration; the delay in pulmonic closure is due to increased RV volume (preload) requiring longer ejection time, not reduced contractility.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presents for evaluation of an incidentally discovered heart sound. Auscultation reveals a low-pitched sound heard best at the apex with the bell of the stethoscope in the left lateral decubitus position. The sound occurs in early diastole, just after S2. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physiologic S3 gallop from rapid ventricular filling in a healthy young adult

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pathologic S4 gallop from decreased ventricular compliance in hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Opening snap of mitral stenosis from a thickened and calcified mitral valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Systolic ejection click from a congenital bicuspid aortic valve abnormality

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mid-systolic click from mitral valve prolapse with myxomatous valve changes

explanation.summary: A low-pitched early diastolic sound at the apex in a healthy young adult is most consistent with a physiologic S3, which results from rapid ventricular filling in a compliant ventricle.
explanation.whyCorrect: S3 is a low-frequency sound heard in early diastole during the rapid filling phase. In young, healthy individuals (particularly under age 30), a physiologic S3 can be normal and reflects rapid deceleration of blood entering a compliant ventricle. It is best heard at the apex with the bell of the stethoscope in the left lateral decubitus position.

whyWrongByChoice.B: S4 occurs in late diastole (just before S1), not early diastole after S2. It results from atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle and is always pathologic in adults.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An opening snap is a brief, high-pitched sound, not a low-pitched sound. It occurs slightly earlier in diastole and is best heard with the diaphragm at the left sternal border.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An ejection click occurs during systole (shortly after S1), not in diastole. It is associated with bicuspid aortic valve or pulmonic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A mid-systolic click occurs during mid-to-late systole, not in diastole. It is the hallmark of mitral valve prolapse, not a normal finding.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with longstanding poorly controlled hypertension presents with exertional dyspnea. On auscultation, a low-pitched sound is heard just before S1, best appreciated at the apex with the bell of the stethoscope. An echocardiogram shows concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with an ejection fraction of 60%. Which of the following best explains the mechanism producing this extra heart sound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid deceleration of blood entering a dilated ventricle during early filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turbulent blood flow across a stenotic aortic valve during ventricular ejection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial contraction forcing blood into a ventricle with decreased compliance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vibration of chordae tendineae during mitral valve closure in early systole

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sudden tensing of the pericardium during rapid ventricular volume expansion

explanation.summary: A sound just before S1 (late diastole) at the apex is S4, produced by forceful atrial contraction against a stiff, noncompliant ventricle, as seen with LVH from chronic hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: S4 is a late diastolic sound produced during atrial systole when the atrium contracts forcefully to push blood into a ventricle with reduced compliance. In this patient, chronic hypertension has caused concentric LVH, making the ventricular wall stiff. The vigorous atrial contraction required to fill this noncompliant ventricle produces the audible S4 gallop.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rapid deceleration of blood during early filling produces S3, not S4. S3 occurs in early diastole after S2, not just before S1 in late diastole.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Turbulent flow across a stenotic aortic valve produces a systolic murmur, not a diastolic gallop sound. This would be heard during ejection, not in late diastole.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vibration of chordae tendineae during valve closure contributes to S1, which occurs at the onset of systole, not the sound described just before S1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pericardial constraint producing sounds is seen in constrictive pericarditis (pericardial knock), which occurs in early diastole and has different characteristics than S4.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic fever presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion. On auscultation, a high-pitched opening snap is heard shortly after S2, followed by a low-pitched rumbling diastolic murmur best heard at the apex. As the severity of her valvular disease worsens over time, the physician would expect the interval between S2 and the opening snap to change. Which of the following changes in this interval indicates worsening disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The S2-OS interval becomes shorter as left atrial pressure increases

choices.label: B
choices.text: The S2-OS interval becomes longer as left atrial pressure increases

choices.label: C
choices.text: The S2-OS interval remains constant regardless of disease severity

choices.label: D
choices.text: The S2-OS interval becomes shorter as left ventricular compliance increases

choices.label: E
choices.text: The S2-OS interval becomes longer as left ventricular pressure decreases

explanation.summary: In mitral stenosis, a shorter S2-OS interval indicates more severe disease because higher left atrial pressure forces the stenotic mitral valve open sooner after aortic valve closure.
explanation.whyCorrect: The opening snap (OS) occurs when the stenotic but still pliable mitral valve opens. As mitral stenosis worsens, left atrial pressure rises progressively. Higher left atrial pressure means that during isovolumetric relaxation, atrial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure sooner, forcing the mitral valve open earlier. This shortens the S2-OS interval. A very short S2-OS interval (< 60 ms) indicates severe mitral stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: As left atrial pressure increases with worsening stenosis, the opening snap occurs sooner (shorter interval), not later. A longer interval would suggest milder disease with lower atrial pressures.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The S2-OS interval is a clinically important marker of severity that changes with disease progression; it does not remain constant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left ventricular compliance is not the primary determinant of the S2-OS interval in mitral stenosis; left atrial pressure relative to ventricular relaxation pressure is the key factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While LV pressure does decrease during isovolumetric relaxation, the primary driver of a shorter interval is higher LA pressure, not lower LV pressure.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents for evaluation of an abnormal heart sound discovered during a prior examination. Auscultation reveals a split S2 that is present during both inspiration and expiration and does not vary with respiration. He has no symptoms and his oxygen saturation is 98%. An echocardiogram shows an atrial septal defect with left-to-right shunting. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of fixed splitting of S2 in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic right ventricular volume overload equalizes filling changes during respiration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left bundle branch block causes delayed left ventricular contraction during systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance delays pulmonic valve closure independent of respiration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paradoxical septal motion causes earlier aortic valve closure during expiration only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased left ventricular preload causes persistently early aortic valve closure

explanation.summary: In ASD, the left-to-right shunt creates chronic RV volume overload. During inspiration, increased venous return to the right heart is offset by decreased shunting across the ASD, keeping RV volume relatively constant throughout the respiratory cycle.
explanation.whyCorrect: In an ASD, the atrial communication allows equalization of respiratory effects on filling. During inspiration, increased systemic venous return to the RA is offset by a proportional decrease in left-to-right shunting (as the RA pressure rises, the pressure gradient across the ASD decreases). During expiration, decreased venous return is offset by increased shunting. The net result is a constant RV volume and consistently delayed P2, producing fixed splitting of S2.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Left bundle branch block causes paradoxical splitting (reversed splitting) of S2, not fixed splitting. In LBBB, delayed LV contraction causes A2 to follow P2.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance alone would cause widened splitting, but it would still vary with respiration unless an ASD is present to equalize filling changes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While paradoxical septal motion can occur in RV volume overload, it does not explain fixed splitting of S2; the mechanism is related to equalization of RV filling across the respiratory cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreased LV preload may contribute to earlier A2, but this alone does not explain why the splitting is fixed; the key mechanism is the equalization of RV filling through the atrial communication.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 20% is evaluated. On auscultation, both S3 and S4 are heard, along with a summation gallop at a heart rate of 110 bpm. The physician explains that at higher heart rates, S3 and S4 can merge. Which of the following heart rates would most likely cause S3 and S4 to merge into a summation gallop?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 60 bpm, because the prolonged diastole allows both sounds to be generated

choices.label: B
choices.text: 75 bpm, because this is the normal resting heart rate for adequate filling

choices.label: C
choices.text: 90 bpm, because moderate tachycardia slightly shortens the filling period

choices.label: D
choices.text: 120 bpm, because shortened diastole brings early and late filling together

choices.label: E
choices.text: 150 bpm, because extreme tachycardia eliminates all diastolic filling phases

explanation.summary: At heart rates around 120 bpm, diastole is shortened enough that the rapid filling phase (S3) and atrial contraction (S4) overlap, producing a single loud summation gallop.
explanation.whyCorrect: A summation gallop occurs when diastole is sufficiently shortened that S3 (early diastole, rapid filling) and S4 (late diastole, atrial contraction) merge into a single loud sound. This typically occurs at heart rates above 100-120 bpm, where the diastolic filling period is compressed enough to bring early and late filling events close together. At 120 bpm, the cycle length is 500 ms and diastole is very brief.

whyWrongByChoice.A: At 60 bpm (1000 ms cycle length), diastole is long enough to clearly separate S3 and S4 into distinct sounds; they would not merge at this rate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: At 75 bpm (800 ms cycle length), diastole is still sufficiently long that S3 and S4 remain temporally separated and would be heard as distinct sounds.
whyWrongByChoice.C: At 90 bpm, diastole is modestly shortened but typically still allows enough separation between early and late filling to keep S3 and S4 distinguishable.
whyWrongByChoice.E: At 150 bpm, while diastole is very short, the question asks about merging of S3 and S4; at very high rates, diastolic filling is so brief that the summation gallop phenomenon is already established at lower rates around 120 bpm.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman undergoes a cardiac examination. The examiner notes that S1 intensity can vary under different physiological conditions. In which of the following scenarios would S1 intensity be expected to be the loudest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged PR interval of 280 ms with the mitral leaflets nearly closed at systole onset

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heavily calcified mitral valve with severely restricted leaflet excursion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severe mitral regurgitation with incomplete coaptation of the valve leaflets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Short PR interval of 120 ms with the mitral leaflets widely separated at systole onset

choices.label: E
choices.text: Low cardiac output state with reduced rate of ventricular pressure generation

explanation.summary: S1 is loudest when the PR interval is short, because the mitral valve leaflets are still widely open when ventricular contraction begins, causing them to snap shut with greater force and excursion.
explanation.whyCorrect: The intensity of S1 depends on the position of the AV valve leaflets at the onset of ventricular contraction and the rate of pressure rise. With a short PR interval (120 ms), there is less time between atrial and ventricular depolarization, so the mitral leaflets are still wide open when the ventricle contracts. The leaflets must travel a greater distance to close, producing a louder S1. This is also why S1 is loud in mitral stenosis (leaflets remain open due to the pressure gradient).

whyWrongByChoice.A: A prolonged PR interval allows the mitral leaflets to drift back toward a nearly closed position before ventricular systole begins, resulting in a soft S1 with minimal leaflet excursion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Severe mitral regurgitation with incomplete coaptation results in a diminished S1 because the leaflets fail to close properly, reducing the closing sound.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A heavily calcified mitral valve has restricted leaflet mobility, reducing the amplitude of valve closure and producing a soft or absent S1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Low cardiac output reduces the rate of ventricular pressure rise (dP/dt), which decreases the force of valve closure and results in a softer S1.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of left bundle branch block is examined. On auscultation, the physician notes that S2 splits during expiration and becomes single during inspiration. This is the reverse of normal physiological splitting. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this paradoxical splitting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delayed right ventricular activation causes late pulmonic valve closure during expiration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Early right ventricular ejection causes premature pulmonic valve closure during inspiration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delayed left ventricular activation causes aortic valve closure to follow pulmonic closure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased pulmonary blood flow prolongs right ventricular ejection during expiration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased systemic vascular resistance shortens left ventricular ejection during inspiration

explanation.summary: In LBBB, delayed left ventricular depolarization and contraction prolongs LV ejection time, causing A2 to occur after P2. During inspiration, increased RV filling delays P2 enough to bring it closer to the delayed A2, narrowing or eliminating the split.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left bundle branch block delays left ventricular depolarization and contraction. This prolongs LV ejection time, causing A2 (aortic component) to occur after P2 (pulmonic component) — reversing the normal order. During expiration, P2 occurs at its earliest timing while A2 remains delayed, producing audible splitting. During inspiration, P2 is delayed (from increased RV filling), moving it closer to or overlapping with A2, reducing or eliminating the split.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In LBBB, the right ventricle is activated normally through the right bundle; it is the left ventricle that has delayed activation, not the right ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Right ventricular ejection timing is not primarily affected by LBBB; pulmonic valve closure timing varies normally with respiration but is not the cause of paradoxical splitting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased pulmonary blood flow is not the mechanism in LBBB; paradoxical splitting results from delayed LV activation, not from changes in pulmonary blood flow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Systemic vascular resistance changes are not the primary mechanism; the paradoxical splitting is caused by delayed electrical activation of the left ventricle, prolonging its ejection time.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with decompensated heart failure is admitted to the hospital. On examination, jugular venous distension, bilateral lower extremity edema, and an S3 gallop are noted. His ejection fraction is 25%. Which of the following pathophysiological mechanisms best explains why S3 is considered pathologic in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forceful atrial contraction against a stiff hypertrophied ventricular wall in diastole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sudden tensing of calcified mitral valve leaflets during the opening snap in diastole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turbulent retrograde flow across an incompetent mitral valve during ventricular systole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vibration of a thickened pericardium constraining ventricular expansion during filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rapid deceleration of blood entering a volume-overloaded dilated ventricle in diastole

explanation.summary: Pathologic S3 in heart failure is caused by rapid blood flow into a dilated, volume-overloaded ventricle during early diastole, with sudden deceleration as the noncompliant ventricular wall reaches its limit of distension.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a patient with systolic heart failure and reduced EF, the ventricle is dilated and volume-overloaded. During early diastolic rapid filling, blood rushes from the atrium (which has elevated pressure) into the ventricle. The sudden deceleration of this blood column as the ventricle reaches its distension limit produces the S3 gallop. This is pathologic and indicates elevated filling pressures and poor systolic function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Forceful atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle produces S4 (atrial gallop), not S3. S4 occurs in late diastole, while S3 occurs in early diastole during rapid filling.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Retrograde flow across the mitral valve during systole produces a mitral regurgitation murmur, not an S3 gallop. Murmurs and gallops have different mechanisms and characteristics.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericardial vibration from constriction produces a pericardial knock, which differs in timing and pitch from S3. It occurs in constrictive pericarditis, not typical heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An opening snap from a thickened mitral valve is a high-pitched brief sound seen in mitral stenosis, distinct from the low-pitched S3 gallop heard in heart failure.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with a prosthetic mechanical mitral valve presents for follow-up. On auscultation, the physician expects to hear specific sounds associated with the prosthetic valve. Compared to a normal native mitral valve, which of the following changes in heart sounds would be expected with a properly functioning mechanical mitral valve prosthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: S1 is softer due to the dampened closure of the rigid prosthetic valve leaflets

choices.label: B
choices.text: S1 has a louder, more metallic quality due to the crisp closure of prosthetic discs

choices.label: C
choices.text: S2 is louder because the prosthetic valve increases left ventricular afterload significantly

choices.label: D
choices.text: An S3 gallop is routinely heard because of altered diastolic filling dynamics

choices.label: E
choices.text: An S4 gallop is always present due to chronic elevation of left atrial pressures

explanation.summary: A properly functioning mechanical mitral valve produces a louder, metallic-quality S1 due to the crisp closure of the rigid prosthetic disc or leaflet components.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mechanical prosthetic valves are made of rigid materials (pyrolytic carbon, metal) that produce a distinctive loud, sharp, metallic closing sound. For a mechanical mitral prosthesis, this metallic closure sound replaces the normal S1, making it louder and more crisp. An opening click may also be heard. Diminution of this metallic closure sound may indicate valve malfunction (e.g., thrombus or pannus).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mechanical valves produce louder, not softer, closure sounds because the rigid prosthetic materials generate sharp, metallic sounds upon closure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A mechanical mitral valve does not significantly affect S2, which is produced by closure of the aortic and pulmonic valves, not the mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An S3 gallop is not routinely produced by a mechanical mitral valve; its presence would suggest volume overload or ventricular dysfunction, not normal prosthetic function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An S4 gallop is not always present with a mechanical mitral valve; its presence would indicate reduced ventricular compliance from other pathology, not normal prosthetic function.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man is found to have a heart sound abnormality during a physical examination. The physician hears a brief, high-pitched early systolic sound immediately after S1, best heard at the second right intercostal space. The sound does not vary with respiration. Echocardiography reveals a congenital valve abnormality. Which of the following conditions is most likely responsible for this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral valve prolapse causing mid-systolic click with late systolic murmur

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bicuspid aortic valve causing an early systolic ejection click at the base

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tricuspid stenosis causing an opening snap best heard at lower sternal border

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonic regurgitation causing a high-pitched decrescendo diastolic murmur

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitral stenosis causing an opening snap followed by a diastolic rumbling murmur

explanation.summary: An early systolic ejection click at the second right intercostal space (aortic area) that does not vary with respiration is characteristic of a bicuspid aortic valve.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ejection clicks are brief, high-pitched sounds that occur in early systole, shortly after S1, when a stenotic or abnormal semilunar valve reaches its maximum opening excursion. An aortic ejection click is best heard at the second right intercostal space (aortic listening area) and characteristically does not change with respiration. A bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital cardiac abnormality and frequently produces an ejection click.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mitral valve prolapse produces a mid-systolic (not early systolic) click, best heard at the apex, not at the second right intercostal space.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tricuspid stenosis produces an opening snap in diastole (not systole) and is best heard at the lower left sternal border, not the second right intercostal space.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonic regurgitation produces a diastolic murmur, not an early systolic click. It is heard at the left upper sternal border, not the right.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitral stenosis produces a diastolic opening snap and murmur, not an early systolic click. It is best heard at the apex, not the second right intercostal space.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic hypertension has developed concentric left ventricular hypertrophy. His left ventricular end-diastolic pressure is elevated at 18 mmHg, but his end-diastolic volume is normal at 120 mL. On the pressure-volume diagram, the end-diastolic pressure-volume relationship (EDPVR) curve has shifted. Which of the following best describes the change in ventricular compliance in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compliance is increased, with a flatter EDPVR curve and lower pressures at any volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compliance is decreased, with a steeper EDPVR curve and higher pressures at any volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compliance is unchanged, with the same EDPVR curve but operating at a different point

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compliance is increased, with a rightward shift allowing greater filling at lower pressures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Compliance is decreased, with a rightward shift allowing greater filling at normal pressures

explanation.summary: Concentric LVH from chronic hypertension decreases ventricular compliance (stiffer ventricle), causing the EDPVR to shift upward and become steeper, resulting in higher filling pressures for any given volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: Compliance is defined as the change in volume per change in pressure (dV/dP). In concentric LVH, the thickened ventricular wall is stiffer, meaning greater pressure is required to achieve the same degree of filling. On the PV diagram, this manifests as an upward and leftward shift of the EDPVR curve (steeper slope). The patient's normal EDV of 120 mL but elevated EDP of 18 mmHg confirms decreased compliance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased compliance would produce a flatter EDPVR curve with lower pressures, which is the opposite of what occurs in concentric LVH where the wall is thickened and stiff.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Compliance is not unchanged; the elevated EDP at normal EDV demonstrates that the pressure-volume relationship itself has changed, indicating reduced compliance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased compliance with a rightward shift is seen in dilated cardiomyopathy (eccentric remodeling), not concentric hypertrophy from chronic pressure overload.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While compliance is decreased, the shift is upward/leftward (not rightward), and the ventricle does not accommodate greater filling; it has elevated pressures at the same or reduced volumes.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with dilated cardiomyopathy is being treated with a positive inotropic agent (dobutamine). Before treatment, her left ventricular end-systolic volume was 150 mL and end-diastolic volume was 220 mL. After dobutamine infusion, her end-diastolic volume remains at 220 mL but end-systolic volume decreases to 110 mL. Which of the following changes on the pressure-volume loop best represents the effect of increased contractility?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR) shifts to the left and upward

choices.label: B
choices.text: The end-diastolic pressure-volume relationship (EDPVR) shifts to the right and downward

choices.label: C
choices.text: The end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR) shifts to the right and downward

choices.label: D
choices.text: The end-diastolic pressure-volume relationship (EDPVR) shifts to the left and upward

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both the ESPVR and EDPVR curves shift rightward simultaneously by equal amounts

explanation.summary: Increased contractility shifts the ESPVR (end-systolic pressure-volume relationship) to the left and upward, indicating that the ventricle can generate higher pressure and eject to a smaller end-systolic volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ESPVR represents the maximum pressure the ventricle can generate at any given volume and is the gold standard index of contractility. Increased contractility (as with dobutamine) shifts the ESPVR line to the left and increases its slope. This means the ventricle achieves a smaller end-systolic volume (110 mL instead of 150 mL) at the same or higher pressure, resulting in increased stroke volume (110 mL vs. 70 mL) and ejection fraction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The EDPVR reflects passive ventricular compliance (diastolic stiffness) and is not directly altered by inotropic agents. Dobutamine acts on contractility, not passive compliance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A rightward shift of the ESPVR would indicate decreased contractility (worse systolic function), which is the opposite of dobutamine's positive inotropic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A leftward upward shift of the EDPVR would indicate decreased compliance (stiffer ventricle), which is not the primary effect of dobutamine. This is seen with LVH or restrictive disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inotropic agents primarily affect the ESPVR, not the EDPVR. The two curves are independent; contractility changes the ESPVR while compliance changes the EDPVR.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with acute decompensated heart failure. His physician starts an intravenous sodium nitroprusside infusion to reduce afterload. Assuming contractility and preload remain constant, which of the following hemodynamic changes would be expected with afterload reduction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased stroke volume, decreased end-systolic volume, and increased ejection fraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased stroke volume, increased end-systolic volume, and decreased ejection fraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased stroke volume, decreased end-systolic volume, and increased ejection fraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased stroke volume, increased end-systolic volume, and decreased ejection fraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Unchanged stroke volume, unchanged end-systolic volume, and unchanged ejection fraction

explanation.summary: Reducing afterload with nitroprusside decreases the resistance against which the ventricle ejects, allowing more complete emptying (decreased ESV), increased stroke volume, and improved ejection fraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Afterload is the wall stress the ventricle must overcome during ejection. Reducing afterload with a vasodilator like nitroprusside decreases aortic impedance, allowing the ventricle to eject more blood. End-systolic volume decreases (more complete emptying), stroke volume increases (SV = EDV - ESV, with EDV constant and ESV decreased), and ejection fraction rises (EF = SV/EDV). On the PV loop, the loop shifts leftward with a smaller ESV.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stroke volume increases, not decreases, with afterload reduction because the ventricle faces less resistance and can eject more blood per beat.
whyWrongByChoice.B: End-systolic volume decreases, not increases, because the ventricle empties more completely against reduced resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both stroke volume and ejection fraction increase with afterload reduction; this answer describes the opposite (increased afterload effect).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Afterload reduction has significant hemodynamic effects; stroke volume and ejection fraction both increase, and end-systolic volume decreases.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man undergoes rapid infusion of 2 liters of intravenous normal saline for volume resuscitation. According to the Frank-Starling mechanism, this intervention primarily increases cardiac output by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing ventricular contractility through enhanced calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreasing afterload by diluting circulating catecholamines and reducing vascular tone significantly

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing myocardial oxygen delivery by improving coronary perfusion pressure and flow reserve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreasing heart rate through a vagal reflex, thereby prolonging diastolic filling time

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increasing preload and sarcomere stretch, optimizing actin-myosin overlap for stronger contraction

explanation.summary: Volume infusion increases preload (venous return and end-diastolic volume), which stretches the sarcomeres toward optimal length, increasing the force of contraction and stroke volume via the Frank-Starling mechanism.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Frank-Starling mechanism states that increased venous return stretches the ventricular myocardium, increasing sarcomere length toward the optimal point on the length-tension curve. This optimizes actin-myosin cross-bridge overlap, increases calcium sensitivity of troponin C, and produces a more forceful contraction with greater stroke volume. Volume infusion directly increases preload (EDV), moving the heart up the Starling curve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The Frank-Starling mechanism operates through changes in sarcomere length, not through increased calcium release. Enhanced calcium release is the mechanism of true positive inotropic agents like dobutamine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Volume infusion does not primarily decrease afterload; if anything, increased intravascular volume may slightly increase blood pressure and afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Volume infusion does not primarily decrease heart rate through a vagal reflex; the primary mechanism of increased cardiac output is through increased stroke volume via the Frank-Starling mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While improved circulating volume may support coronary perfusion, this is not the Frank-Starling mechanism. The Starling mechanism specifically relates preload (sarcomere stretch) to contractile force.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis has a peak aortic valve gradient of 60 mmHg. Her left ventricular systolic pressure is 210 mmHg while her systolic blood pressure is 150 mmHg. Compared to a patient with normal aortic valve function and similar blood pressure, which of the following best describes the change in left ventricular afterload in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Afterload is decreased because the stenotic valve reduces the velocity of ejected blood

choices.label: B
choices.text: Afterload is unchanged because systemic blood pressure is the sole determinant of afterload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Afterload is increased because the ventricle must generate higher pressure to eject across the stenotic valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Afterload is decreased because the small valve orifice reduces the volume of blood ejected

choices.label: E
choices.text: Afterload is unchanged because compensatory hypertrophy normalizes ventricular wall stress

explanation.summary: In severe aortic stenosis, the ventricle must generate much higher systolic pressure (210 mmHg) to overcome the valvular obstruction, significantly increasing afterload (wall stress during ejection).
explanation.whyCorrect: Afterload is the wall stress the ventricle faces during ejection, determined by the Law of Laplace (wall stress = Pressure x radius / 2 x wall thickness). In aortic stenosis, the ventricle must generate a much higher systolic pressure (210 mmHg vs. normal ~130 mmHg) to push blood across the stenotic valve. Despite the systemic BP being 150 mmHg, the LV experiences afterload based on the pressure it must generate (210 mmHg), not the downstream aortic pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Reduced velocity of ejected blood does not decrease afterload; the ventricle must generate higher pressure to overcome the stenosis, which increases wall stress and afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Systemic blood pressure is not the sole determinant of LV afterload; in aortic stenosis, the valve obstruction adds significant resistance that increases the pressure the ventricle must generate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While the stenotic valve does limit flow, the reduced ejection volume does not decrease afterload; the ventricle faces markedly increased pressure load, which is the primary determinant of afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While compensatory concentric hypertrophy increases wall thickness and partially normalizes wall stress (Laplace), it does not fully normalize afterload. The markedly elevated LV pressure still represents increased afterload.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with no prior cardiac history is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident with significant blood loss. His blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg and heart rate is 130 bpm. Despite maximal sympathetic activation, his cardiac output remains low. On a Frank-Starling curve, where would this patient's ventricle be operating compared to normal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At a higher point on the same Starling curve due to increased contractility from catecholamines

choices.label: B
choices.text: At a lower point on the same Starling curve due to decreased preload from blood loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: On a depressed Starling curve at the same preload due to myocardial depression from hemorrhage

choices.label: D
choices.text: On an elevated Starling curve at a lower preload due to increased contractility offsetting volume loss

choices.label: E
choices.text: At the flat portion of the normal Starling curve indicating maximal preload reserve utilization

explanation.summary: In hemorrhagic shock with intact cardiac function, preload is reduced (moving leftward on the curve) but sympathetic activation shifts the patient to a higher Starling curve (increased contractility). The net effect is operation on an elevated curve at a lower preload.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has hemorrhagic shock with two competing effects: (1) blood loss decreases preload, shifting the operating point leftward along the Starling curve; (2) maximal sympathetic activation increases contractility (via catecholamines), shifting the entire Starling curve upward. The net result is that the patient operates on an elevated Starling curve but at a markedly reduced preload. Despite the enhanced contractility, the severely reduced preload limits cardiac output.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While catecholamines do increase contractility, the patient is not simply at a higher point on the same curve; both preload change (leftward shift) and the curve itself shifts upward (increased contractility).
whyWrongByChoice.B: This answer correctly identifies decreased preload but ignores the sympathetic activation, which shifts the patient to a higher Starling curve, not the same one.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no myocardial depression in this patient with hemorrhagic shock and previously normal cardiac function; the heart itself is functioning with enhanced contractility from catecholamines.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The flat portion of the Starling curve represents high preload states; this patient has markedly reduced preload from hemorrhage, placing him on the steep ascending limb.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with chronic systolic heart failure has an ejection fraction of 25%. Her cardiologist explains that the Law of Laplace relates wall stress to ventricular geometry. She has a dilated left ventricle with an internal radius of 4 cm and a wall thickness of 0.8 cm. Compared to a normal left ventricle with a radius of 2.5 cm and wall thickness of 1.0 cm, at the same intraventricular pressure, how does this patient's wall stress differ?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wall stress is approximately the same because pressure is the dominant determinant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wall stress is decreased because the thinner wall distributes force over a larger area

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wall stress is markedly increased due to the larger radius and thinner ventricular wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wall stress is decreased because dilation improves sarcomere overlap and efficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: Wall stress is unchanged because compensatory mechanisms fully normalize wall tension

explanation.summary: By the Law of Laplace (wall stress = P x r / 2h), the dilated ventricle has both increased radius and decreased wall thickness, dramatically increasing wall stress compared to normal.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Law of Laplace states that wall stress (sigma) = (P x r) / (2 x h), where P is pressure, r is radius, and h is wall thickness. For this patient: stress is proportional to (P x 4) / (2 x 0.8) = 2.5P. For normal: stress is proportional to (P x 2.5) / (2 x 1.0) = 1.25P. At the same pressure, her wall stress is approximately double that of normal. The increased radius and decreased wall thickness both contribute to elevated afterload.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While pressure is important, the Law of Laplace clearly shows that radius and wall thickness are also critical determinants; the geometric changes in this patient markedly increase wall stress.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The thinner wall actually increases wall stress per unit area, not decreases it. A thinner wall must bear the same total force over less tissue, increasing stress per unit area.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dilation beyond optimal sarcomere length actually impairs sarcomere overlap and reduces contractile efficiency; it does not decrease wall stress.
whyWrongByChoice.E: In dilated cardiomyopathy, compensatory mechanisms (such as eccentric hypertrophy) are insufficient to fully normalize wall stress; the increased radius-to-thickness ratio maintains elevated afterload.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with chronic heart failure is started on carvedilol, a combined alpha-1 and beta-adrenergic blocker. After 6 months of therapy, his ejection fraction has improved from 25% to 40%, and his left ventricular end-diastolic dimension has decreased from 6.5 cm to 5.5 cm. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of long-term benefit of beta-blocker therapy in systolic heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute reduction in heart rate increases diastolic filling time and immediately raises stroke volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prevention of catecholamine-induced myocardial toxicity allows reverse ventricular remodeling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct positive inotropic effect of beta-blockers increases contractility and improves emptying

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduction in afterload through peripheral vasodilation is the sole mechanism of improvement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased myocardial oxygen consumption improves aerobic metabolism and contractile performance

explanation.summary: Long-term beta-blocker therapy in heart failure protects the myocardium from chronic catecholamine toxicity, allowing reverse remodeling with decreased ventricular dilation and improved contractility over months.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic heart failure, compensatory neurohormonal activation leads to sustained catecholamine excess, which causes myocardial toxicity (oxidative stress, apoptosis, calcium overload, beta-receptor downregulation, and fibrosis). Beta-blockers block these deleterious effects, allowing reverse remodeling over months: the ventricle becomes smaller, contractility improves, and ejection fraction increases. This explains the improvement from EF 25% to 40% and the decrease in chamber size.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While heart rate reduction does improve filling, the primary long-term benefit is reverse remodeling from protection against catecholamine toxicity, not simply improved diastolic filling.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-blockers are negative inotropes acutely; they do not have a direct positive inotropic effect. The improvement in contractility occurs indirectly through reverse remodeling over months.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While carvedilol has alpha-blocking vasodilatory properties, afterload reduction alone does not explain the structural reverse remodeling (decreased ventricular dimensions and improved EF) seen over months.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-blockers decrease myocardial oxygen consumption (by reducing heart rate and contractility), not increase it. This is part of their benefit, reducing the metabolic burden on the failing heart.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man undergoes cardiac catheterization. At rest, his left ventricular end-diastolic volume is 130 mL and end-systolic volume is 50 mL. He is then given a phenylephrine infusion, which increases his systemic vascular resistance. Assuming his contractility and preload remain unchanged, which of the following changes would be expected on his next cardiac cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: End-systolic volume decreases to 40 mL with an increased ejection fraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: End-systolic volume increases to 65 mL with a decreased stroke volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: End-diastolic volume increases to 160 mL with an unchanged stroke volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: End-diastolic volume decreases to 110 mL with an increased ejection fraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both end-diastolic and end-systolic volumes decrease with unchanged stroke volume

explanation.summary: Phenylephrine increases afterload (SVR). With unchanged contractility and preload, the ventricle cannot eject as completely, leading to increased end-systolic volume and decreased stroke volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-1 agonist that increases systemic vascular resistance (afterload). With increased afterload and unchanged contractility, the ventricle faces greater resistance to ejection and cannot empty as completely. End-systolic volume increases (e.g., from 50 to 65 mL). Since EDV remains at 130 mL (preload unchanged), stroke volume decreases from 80 mL to 65 mL, and ejection fraction falls from 62% to 50%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased afterload impedes ejection, causing ESV to increase, not decrease. Ejection fraction decreases because the ventricle retains more blood at end-systole.
whyWrongByChoice.C: On the immediate next beat with unchanged preload, EDV does not increase to 160 mL. Over subsequent beats, the increased ESV may slightly increase the next EDV through the Frank-Starling mechanism, but not to this extent acutely.
whyWrongByChoice.D: EDV does not decrease with afterload increase; if anything, it may slightly increase over subsequent beats due to retained volume. Ejection fraction decreases, not increases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: End-systolic volume increases (not decreases) with increased afterload. End-diastolic volume is maintained by the stated unchanged preload.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with a history of two prior myocardial infarctions has an ejection fraction of 20%. On a Frank-Starling curve, his ventricle operates on a flat, depressed curve. The physician administers a 500 mL bolus of intravenous saline. Which of the following best describes the expected effect of this fluid bolus on his cardiac output and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiac output increases substantially with a modest rise in wedge pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac output increases modestly with a substantial rise in wedge pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac output remains unchanged with a substantial rise in wedge pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiac output decreases substantially with a minimal change in wedge pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both cardiac output and wedge pressure remain essentially unchanged after the bolus

explanation.summary: On a flat, depressed Starling curve, additional volume (preload) produces little increase in stroke volume but significantly raises filling pressures, risking pulmonary congestion.
explanation.whyCorrect: A patient with an EF of 20% and prior infarctions operates on the flat portion of a depressed Frank-Starling curve. On this plateau, additional preload (fluid bolus) produces minimal increase in stroke volume and cardiac output because the ventricle is already near its maximal output for its level of contractility. However, the increased volume substantially raises filling pressures (PCWP), potentially causing pulmonary edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: On a flat, depressed Starling curve, the ventricle cannot substantially increase cardiac output with additional preload; a large CO increase would only occur on the steep ascending portion of a normal Starling curve.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac output does increase modestly (not zero change) because even on the flat portion of the curve, there is a small incremental gain in stroke volume with added preload.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac output does not decrease with added preload in this scenario; while the ventricle is severely impaired, additional volume does not cause acute contractile depression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Wedge pressure definitely rises with added volume; the failing ventricle has reduced compliance and cannot accommodate additional volume without significant pressure increases.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old healthy woman is undergoing physiological testing. Her resting cardiac output is 5 L/min. She begins vigorous exercise on a treadmill, and her cardiac output increases to 20 L/min. Her heart rate increases from 70 to 170 bpm.
